stories.headline: Police Gunfight With Rio Favela Gang Traps 200 Tourists on Hilltop Overlook
stories.summary:
Approximately 200 tourists were stranded atop Morro Dois Irmãos, a popular hilltop overlooking Ipanema Beach in Rio de Janeiro, Monday morning after a police operation against the Comando Vermelho gang in the adjacent Vidigal favela erupted into a gunfight and blocked the main access trail. Officers from the Public Prosecutor's Office of Bahia and Rio's Polícia Civil were seeking to arrest alleged gang members believed to be hiding in the neighborhood when shooting broke out.

Video circulated on social media showing the stranded group sitting on the ground at sunrise while a police helicopter circled overhead and gunfire could be heard in the distance. Tourists from Portugal and Brazil described being told by guides to sit down and remain calm as the exchange of fire continued below. The group was able to descend safely after approximately 30 minutes once police secured the route.

Local guides told broadcaster TV Globo they had been informed of the operation in advance and coordinated with police units in Vidigal, though at least some groups were caught off guard when the gunfight began before they could descend. No tourists were reported injured in the incident.

Comando Vermelho and similar criminal organizations in Rio control not only the drug trade in the areas they occupy but also hold monopolies over the provision of gas, cable television, internet, and transport services, making anti-gang operations in favelas a persistent challenge for Brazilian law enforcement.

stories.headline: Vivek Ramaswamy Nearly Tied With Democrat Amy Acton in Ohio Governor's Race: Poll
stories.summary:
A new poll from Bowling Green State University found Republican frontrunner Vivek Ramaswamy in a statistical dead heat with presumptive Democratic nominee Amy Acton in the 2026 Ohio gubernatorial race, with 48 percent of registered voters backing Ramaswamy in the survey of 1,000 respondents. The tight numbers signal a competitive general election in a state that has trended Republican in recent cycles but where Democrats see an opportunity with a well-known candidate.

Acton, a physician and former Ohio Department of Health director who became a prominent public face during the COVID-19 pandemic, is the likely Democratic nominee. Ramaswamy, the entrepreneur and former 2024 Republican presidential candidate who briefly led the Department of Government Efficiency before departing, secured endorsements that have made him the clear frontrunner for the GOP nomination.

The poll result is notable given Ohio's recent rightward shift, with Republicans having won statewide offices by substantial margins in recent cycles. A competitive race would require Democrats to mobilize urban and suburban voters while Ramaswamy works to consolidate the Republican base and appeal to independents.

Separately, in California's gubernatorial primary, former State Controller Betty Yee announced Monday she is suspending her campaign, citing lagging poll numbers and limited time before the primary. Her exit is seen as potentially benefiting Xavier Becerra, who has gained support following the withdrawal of Rep. Eric Swalwell amid sexual harassment allegations.

stories.headline: Goldman Environmental Prize Awarded to Six Women for First Time in 37-Year History
stories.summary:
Nigerian ecologist Iroro Tanshi has won the 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize for her work protecting endangered short-tailed roundleaf bats in southeastern Nigeria's Afi Mountain Wildlife Sanctuary, in what organizers called a historic year for the award: for the first time in the prize's 37-year history, all six global winners are women. Tanshi, currently a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Washington, said the honor signals that her work has 'global relevance.'

Tanshi's campaign began after she rediscovered the short-tailed roundleaf bat — not seen in nearly 50 years — only to face an immediate threat from wildfires burning near the sanctuary. She built a community fire brigade program, framing wildfire prevention as a shared community need rather than a conservation cause alone, which she credited with winning local cooperation. The brigades helped prevent serious wildfires across the 24,700-acre sanctuary between 2022 and 2025.

A significant challenge in her work has been overcoming deeply held cultural associations between bats and witchcraft in Nigeria, where the animals are widely feared. Tanshi and her team engaged communities through multiple media platforms with a particular focus on children, emphasizing the ecological roles bats play, including seed dispersal and pollination of economically important plants such as the shea butter tree.

The Goldman Environmental Prize, sometimes called the 'Green Nobel,' is awarded annually to grassroots environmental activists from six continental regions. The all-female lineup of winners this year was described by organizers as a first in the prize's history.

stories.headline: Fish Takes Center Stage in West Bengal Election as BJP and TMC Clash Over Cultural Identity
stories.summary:
Candidates in India's upcoming West Bengal state assembly elections are campaigning with live fish in hand as a political symbol, seeking to counter the perception that the Hindu-nationalist Bharatiya Janata Party would restrict the fish-eating habits central to Bengali cultural identity. The BJP, often associated in the public mind with vegetarian-leaning policies and cow protection activism in other states, is working to neutralize those associations in a state where fish and rice are staples of daily life and ritual.

Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee of the ruling Trinamool Congress, seeking a fourth consecutive term, has warned voters that the BJP 'threatens Bengal's way of life,' specifically invoking fish and meat as cultural touchstones the opposition would curtail. 'The BJP will not allow you to eat fish. Nor will they allow you to eat meat or eggs,' Banerjee told a campaign rally.

BJP leaders have sharply rejected those claims as fabrications intended to distract from what they describe as corruption under Banerjee's government. Senior BJP figure Smriti Irani called the charge 'a lie,' while Prime Minister Narendra Modi — himself a vegetarian — turned the fish issue around by accusing the Banerjee government of failing to make West Bengal self-reliant in fish production after 15 years in power.

The episode illustrates how food habits can become proxies for broader debates about regional identity, cultural autonomy, and national political movements in India's diverse and pluralistic democracy. West Bengal, home to more than 100 million people, is one of the few major states where the TMC has successfully resisted BJP's nationwide expansion.
